failure_modes.name: Temperature monitoring and cold chain failure
failure_modes.description: The daily temperature logging and cold-chain verification process has broken down, meaning fridges, freezers, and display units are running without confirmed safe temperatures. This is a root cause because unmonitored temperature drift silently renders food unsafe before any visible spoilage occurs. Unlike FM008 (storage organisation) or FM004 (cross-contamination), this FM targets the monitoring system itself: the absence of calibrated probes, twice-daily checks, and excursion protocols.

failure_modes.name: Allergen management and PPDS compliance failure
failure_modes.description: Allergen information is either missing, inaccurate, or not presented in compliance with Natasha's Law PPDS requirements for items packed on-site. This is a root cause because incorrect allergen data at the point of sale creates direct customer harm potential that no downstream check can catch. Unlike FM004 (physical cross-contamination) or FM007 (HACCP documentation), this FM specifically targets the labelling, communication, and menu-matrix systems that inform customers about what they are eating.

failure_modes.name: Personal hygiene and handwashing discipline failure
failure_modes.description: Staff are not consistently following handwashing protocols at critical control points: after breaks, between tasks, before food prep, and after handling waste or cash. This is a root cause because hygiene discipline is a behavioural system failure, not an equipment or knowledge gap. Unlike FM005 (cleaning schedules) which covers surface and equipment sanitation, this FM is about the personal hygiene habits of individual team members and whether the culture enforces them.

failure_modes.name: Cross-contamination prevention failure
failure_modes.description: Physical barriers and procedural controls to prevent allergen transfer, raw-to-cooked contact, and dietary-category mixing are absent or not enforced. This is a root cause because cross-contamination is a process-design failure in how the kitchen separates, stores, and handles different food categories simultaneously. Unlike FM002 (allergen labelling) which addresses information accuracy, this FM targets the physical workspace practices: separate boards, utensils, storage zones, and workflow sequencing.

failure_modes.name: Cleaning and sanitation programme failure
failure_modes.description: The documented cleaning schedule either does not exist or is not being followed, leaving surfaces, equipment, and preparation areas inconsistently sanitised. This is a root cause because cleaning is a preventive control system; when the schedule breaks down, contamination risk accumulates invisibly until an incident or inspection reveals it. Unlike FM003 (personal hygiene) which targets individual behaviour, this FM covers the programmatic framework: what gets cleaned, how often, by whom, and how completion is verified.

failure_modes.name: Pest control and environmental hygiene failure
failure_modes.description: Pest prevention measures have failed, meaning either no professional pest control contract exists, monitoring stations are not checked, or evidence of pest activity is going unaddressed. This is a root cause because pest ingress is an environmental control failure driven by building maintenance, waste management, and proofing gaps. Unlike FM005 (cleaning programme) which addresses sanitation routines, this FM specifically targets the structural and contractual defences against rodents, insects, and birds.

failure_modes.name: Food safety management system (HACCP) failure
failure_modes.description: No documented HACCP-based food safety management system is in place, or the existing system has not been reviewed and does not reflect current menu items and processes. This is a root cause because HACCP is the overarching framework that identifies critical control points; without it, individual food safety controls operate without systematic logic. Unlike FM009 (training) or FM010 (audit readiness), this FM targets the management system architecture itself: the hazard analysis, CCPs, monitoring procedures, and corrective actions.

failure_modes.name: Food storage and date management failure
failure_modes.description: Food storage practices have broken down: items lack date labels, use-by dates are not checked systematically, FIFO rotation is not enforced, and stock is stored improperly. This is a root cause because poor storage management causes food to degrade or expire undetected, creating both safety risk and financial waste. Unlike FM001 (temperature monitoring) which tracks equipment performance, this FM targets the human systems of labelling, rotation, and shelf-life management that keep stored food safe and usable.

failure_modes.name: Food safety training and certification failure
failure_modes.description: Team members handling food lack current Level 2 food hygiene certification, and no system exists to track certification expiry or schedule refresher training. This is a root cause because untrained staff make unsafe decisions not out of negligence but out of ignorance, and no amount of SOPs compensates for missing foundational knowledge. Unlike FM007 (HACCP system) which targets documentation, this FM addresses whether the people doing the work have the legally required competence and whether the business tracks it.

failure_modes.name: EHO readiness and audit compliance failure
failure_modes.description: The cafe would not pass an unannounced Environmental Health Officer inspection, with gaps across documentation, record-keeping, and visible compliance indicators. This is a root cause because EHO readiness is a systems-integration failure: it means multiple food safety subsystems (temperature logs, cleaning records, training certificates, HACCP documents) are not maintained to inspection standard. Unlike individual FMs for specific controls, this FM targets the audit-readiness discipline of keeping all evidence current, accessible, and complete.

failure_modes.name: Queue management and wait time failure
failure_modes.description: [SYMPTOM-FM] Queue lengths and customer wait times have exceeded acceptable thresholds, but this is a composite outcome driven by upstream failures in staffing levels (FM040), workflow layout (FM041), ticket speed (FM042), and stock readiness (FM044). The description is retained as a diagnostic aggregator because queue problems are the most visible customer-facing signal and cafe owners identify them first. When this FM fires, investigation should trace backward to which upstream root cause is the actual driver rather than treating the queue itself as the thing to fix.

failure_modes.name: Order accuracy and fulfilment failure
failure_modes.description: Orders are being prepared incorrectly: wrong milk alternatives, missing modifications, incorrect food items, or substitutions made without customer confirmation. This is a root cause because order accuracy failures stem from a breakdown in the communication chain between order-taking, ticket creation, and preparation execution. Unlike FM011 (queue management) which is about throughput speed, this FM targets the fidelity of information transfer from customer request to finished product.

failure_modes.name: Customer greeting and service warmth failure
failure_modes.description: Customers are not greeted on entry, not acknowledged at the counter, or experience impersonal transactional service that lacks warmth. This is a root cause because service warmth is a cultural and training failure: staff either have not been taught the expected greeting standard or the culture does not reinforce it. Unlike FM014 (complaint resolution) which handles service failures after they occur, this FM targets the proactive first-impression behaviour that sets customer expectations for the entire visit.

failure_modes.name: Complaint resolution and service recovery failure
failure_modes.description: When service failures or product issues occur, there is no structured process for acknowledgement, resolution, and recovery, leaving complaints unresolved or handled inconsistently. This is a root cause because complaint resolution is a system-design failure: without a defined protocol, each team member improvises, leading to inconsistent outcomes and escalation. Unlike FM013 (greeting warmth) which prevents problems through first impressions, this FM targets the recovery framework that turns service failures into retention opportunities.

failure_modes.name: Customer retention and return visit failure
failure_modes.description: [SYMPTOM-FM] Customer retention and repeat visit frequency are declining, but this is a composite outcome of multiple upstream failures including service warmth (FM013), complaint handling (FM014), product quality (FM023), environment (FM076), and loyalty systems (FM084). This FM is retained as a diagnostic aggregator because retention decline is the highest-consequence commercial signal a cafe can observe. When this FM fires, the investigation should decompose which specific experience failures are driving customers away rather than applying generic loyalty tactics.

failure_modes.name: Accessibility and inclusivity failure
failure_modes.description: The cafe has not addressed physical accessibility, family-friendliness, or inclusive design for customers with disabilities, pushchairs, or specific needs. This is a root cause because accessibility failure is a design and policy omission: the space, furniture, menu, and service approach were not evaluated through an inclusivity lens. Unlike FM078 (seating comfort) which addresses general layout, this FM specifically targets whether the cafe is usable by all customer segments including those with mobility, sensory, or dietary requirements.

failure_modes.name: Takeaway and delivery experience failure
failure_modes.description: The takeaway and delivery customer experience is significantly worse than dine-in: packaging leaks, drinks arrive cold, presentation is poor, or the ordering process is confusing. This is a root cause because takeaway experience failure is a packaging-and-process design gap: the cafe optimised for dine-in and bolted on takeaway without redesigning the product and workflow for off-premises consumption. Unlike FM047 (delivery workflow integration) which addresses operational disruption, this FM targets the end-customer experience quality of the off-premises product.

failure_modes.name: Customer feedback and insight capture failure
failure_modes.description: No structured mechanism exists to collect, analyse, and act on customer feedback, meaning the cafe operates without systematic insight into customer satisfaction. This is a root cause because feedback capture failure is an information-system gap: without input, the business cannot identify problems until they manifest as revenue decline. Unlike FM014 (complaint resolution) which handles individual incidents, this FM targets the ongoing listening infrastructure: surveys, comment cards, review monitoring, and the rhythm of acting on what customers say.

failure_modes.name: Espresso extraction and shot quality failure
failure_modes.description: Espresso shots are being pulled with inconsistent dose, yield, or extraction time, producing sour, bitter, or flat shots that vary between baristas and throughout the day. This is a root cause because extraction failure stems from a calibration discipline gap: the recipe parameters (dose in, liquid out, time) are either undefined or not enforced. Unlike FM020 (grinder calibration) which targets the equipment input, this FM targets the shot-pulling technique, recipe adherence, and quality-checking behaviour at the espresso machine.

failure_modes.name: Grinder calibration and maintenance failure
failure_modes.description: The grinder is not being calibrated at opening, after bean hopper changes, or as environmental conditions shift throughout the day, causing particle-size drift that degrades extraction. This is a root cause because grinder calibration is the single largest variable in espresso quality, and when it drifts, every shot pulled is compromised regardless of barista technique. Unlike FM019 (extraction technique) which targets human skill, this FM targets the equipment-maintenance discipline of keeping the grinder dialled in as a precondition for good coffee.

failure_modes.name: Milk craft and steaming quality failure
failure_modes.description: Milk is being steamed inconsistently: overheated, under-textured, lacking microfoam, or producing flat latte art that indicates poor technique. This is a root cause because milk craft failure is a skill-and-training gap: baristas either were not taught proper steaming technique or are not practising it consistently under time pressure. Unlike FM027 (drink presentation) which covers overall visual standards, this FM specifically targets the technical skill of transforming milk into properly textured microfoam at the correct temperature.

failure_modes.name: Water quality and filtration failure
failure_modes.description: Water used for espresso extraction and brewing is not filtered, not tested for TDS, or affected by limescale, directly compromising coffee flavour and shortening equipment life. This is a root cause because water quality is an infrastructure failure: it is the single most overlooked variable in coffee quality, accounting for over 98% of brewed coffee by volume. Unlike FM043 (equipment maintenance) which addresses mechanical reliability, this FM targets the chemical composition of the water itself and whether filtration systems are installed, maintained, and monitored.

failure_modes.name: Food quality and consistency failure
failure_modes.description: Food items are inconsistent in portion size, presentation, taste, or freshness, varying unpredictably between days, shifts, or team members. This is a root cause because food quality inconsistency is a standardisation and supervision failure: either recipes are not documented, or the preparation process lacks quality checkpoints. Unlike FM025 (recipe standardisation) which targets the documentation system, this FM targets the observed output: whether what reaches the customer is actually consistent, regardless of which processes exist on paper.

failure_modes.name: Menu stagnation and innovation failure
failure_modes.description: The menu has not been refreshed, seasonally updated, or creatively developed in six or more months, leading to customer fatigue and missed commercial opportunities. This is a root cause because menu stagnation is a planning and innovation failure: the business lacks a calendar-driven menu development process. Unlike FM023 (food quality consistency) which targets execution of the current menu, this FM targets whether the menu itself evolves to maintain customer interest, reflect seasonal availability, and capture new revenue.

failure_modes.name: Recipe standardisation and portion control failure
failure_modes.description: No standardised recipes, portion specifications, or drink-build cards exist, or they exist but are not followed, causing cost and quality variation between team members. This is a root cause because recipe standardisation failure is a documentation and enforcement gap: without written standards, each team member produces their own interpretation. Unlike FM023 (food quality) which observes the inconsistent output, this FM targets the missing input: the recipe cards, portion guides, and build standards that should eliminate variation at source.

failure_modes.name: Coffee waste and sustainability failure
failure_modes.description: Coffee waste volumes are not tracked, and there is no system to monitor discarded shots, expired batch brew, unused grounds, or over-production during quiet periods. This is a root cause because untracked waste is a measurement-system failure: you cannot reduce what you do not measure, and coffee waste is often the largest hidden cost in a specialty cafe. Unlike FM048 (general waste tracking) which covers food and packaging waste, this FM specifically targets coffee-specific waste streams and their connection to purchasing, preparation, and demand forecasting.

failure_modes.name: Drink presentation and visual standards failure
failure_modes.description: Drink presentation is inconsistent: latte art quality varies, cup selection is haphazard, and the visual standard of drinks served does not match the cafe's brand positioning. This is a root cause because presentation failure is a standards-definition and enforcement gap: either the expected visual standard has not been defined, or baristas are not held accountable to it. Unlike FM021 (milk steaming technique) which targets the skill prerequisite, this FM targets the final visual output and whether the cafe has established and maintains a presentation standard.

failure_modes.name: Dietary inclusivity and alternative options failure
failure_modes.description: The menu offers insufficient options for customers with vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, or other dietary requirements relative to actual customer demand. This is a root cause because dietary inclusivity failure is a menu-planning omission: the business has not assessed demand for alternative options or has not invested in developing them. Unlike FM016 (general accessibility) which covers physical and service inclusivity, this FM specifically targets the food and drink menu's ability to serve customers with dietary restrictions without making them feel like an afterthought.

failure_modes.name: Staff turnover and retention failure
failure_modes.description: [SYMPTOM-FM] Staff are leaving at elevated rates, particularly within the first six months, but turnover is a composite outcome of failures in onboarding (FM030), progression (FM035), morale (FM036), owner delegation (FM037), and rota fairness (FM038). This FM is retained as a diagnostic aggregator because turnover rate is the most measurable and commercially consequential workforce signal. When this FM fires, the investigation should determine which specific upstream failure is driving departures rather than treating retention as a standalone problem to solve with pay increases alone.

failure_modes.name: Onboarding and induction failure
failure_modes.description: New team members are thrown into service without a structured induction covering cafe standards, food safety requirements, equipment operation, and cultural expectations. This is a root cause because onboarding failure means each new hire develops their own understanding of the job through trial and error, embedding inconsistency from day one. Unlike FM031 (barista skills development) which targets ongoing craft progression, this FM targets the critical first-week experience that determines whether a new hire becomes effective or overwhelmed and leaves.

failure_modes.name: Barista skills and craft development failure
failure_modes.description: Baristas lack core craft competencies: they cannot calibrate the grinder, assess extraction quality, steam milk properly, or articulate coffee knowledge to customers. This is a root cause because skills gaps represent a training-investment failure: the business has not built a structured progression from novice to competent barista. Unlike FM030 (onboarding) which covers initial induction, this FM targets the ongoing skill-development pathway that takes a new barista from basic competence to craft mastery over months.

failure_modes.name: Team communication and meeting failure
failure_modes.description: The team has no regular communication forums: no daily huddles, weekly meetings, or structured channels for sharing information between shifts and roles. This is a root cause because communication failure is an organisational-design gap: without deliberate forums, information travels by rumour, repetition, or not at all. Unlike FM045 (shift handover) which targets the specific transition between shifts, this FM targets the broader communication architecture: whether the team has regular, structured opportunities to align on priorities, share updates, and raise concerns.

failure_modes.name: Performance management and review failure
failure_modes.description: No regular performance reviews, one-to-ones, or structured feedback conversations happen between managers and team members. This is a root cause because performance management failure means underperformance goes unaddressed and strong performance goes unrecognised, both of which erode standards and motivation over time. Unlike FM035 (career pathways) which targets progression structure, this FM targets the feedback loop: whether individuals receive regular, honest assessment of their work and clear guidance on what to improve.

failure_modes.name: Key person dependency and cover failure
failure_modes.description: Critical knowledge, skills, or customer relationships are concentrated in one or two individuals, and the business has no plan for their absence or departure. This is a root cause because key-person dependency is a cross-training and documentation failure: the business has allowed expertise to accumulate in single points of failure rather than distributing it. Unlike FM029 (turnover) which measures departure rates, this FM targets the structural vulnerability that makes any single departure catastrophic, regardless of whether turnover is high or low.

failure_modes.name: Career pathway and progression failure
failure_modes.description: No defined progression pathway exists from barista to senior barista, shift lead, or manager, leaving ambitious team members with no visible future in the business. This is a root cause because career pathway failure is an organisational-design omission: without defined levels, criteria, and rewards, talented staff will seek progression elsewhere. Unlike FM033 (performance reviews) which targets the feedback mechanism, this FM targets the destination: whether there is a visible, achievable next step for someone who wants to grow within the cafe.

failure_modes.name: Team morale and wellbeing failure
failure_modes.description: Team morale is observably low: staff appear disengaged, breaks are skipped, energy is flat, and there is a sense of going-through-the-motions rather than genuine engagement. This is a root cause because low morale is a workplace-environment failure driven by a combination of overwork, under-recognition, poor communication, and lack of purpose. Unlike FM029 (turnover) which measures the consequence of departure, this FM targets the lived experience of staff who are still present but no longer invested, which degrades service quality before anyone actually leaves.

failure_modes.name: Owner overwork and delegation failure
failure_modes.description: The owner or founder is working every shift, has no protected time for strategic thinking, and has not delegated operational authority to the team. This is a root cause because owner overwork is a delegation and trust failure: the business has grown beyond one person's capacity but the operating model has not evolved to match. Unlike FM034 (key-person dependency) which targets any individual's concentrated knowledge, this FM specifically targets the owner's inability to step back, which prevents the business from developing the management layer it needs to be sustainable.

failure_modes.name: Rota planning and shift management failure
failure_modes.description: The staff rota is published late, shift allocation is perceived as unfair, and there is no system for managing holiday requests, swaps, or short-notice changes. This is a root cause because rota failure is a planning-system breakdown: without structured scheduling, staff cannot plan their lives, resentment builds, and coverage gaps appear at the last minute. Unlike FM040 (peak staffing) which targets whether enough people are on shift during rushes, this FM targets the upstream planning process that determines who works when and whether the system feels fair.

failure_modes.name: Opening and closing routine failure
failure_modes.description: Opening and closing routines are not standardised: different people do different things, steps get skipped, and there is no checklist or accountability for daily setup and shutdown. This is a root cause because routine failure is a process-documentation gap: without defined checklists, the quality of each day's start and finish depends entirely on who is working. Unlike FM045 (shift handover) which targets communication between shifts, this FM targets the defined sequence of tasks that bookend every trading day and ensure the cafe opens ready and closes properly.

failure_modes.name: Morning rush and peak management failure
failure_modes.description: The morning rush period (typically 7-10am) is poorly managed: staffing is insufficient, stations are not pre-set, and the team is overwhelmed by predictable demand. This is a root cause because peak management failure is a planning and preparation gap: the rush is entirely predictable yet the business is not configured to handle it. Unlike FM038 (rota planning) which targets the scheduling system, this FM targets the operational readiness for the specific high-pressure window: pre-shift prep, station assignment, and the choreography of peak-hour service.

failure_modes.name: Workflow layout and station design failure
failure_modes.description: The physical layout of the counter, machine, preparation area, and service point creates unnecessary movement, bottlenecks, or workflow collisions during service. This is a root cause because layout failure is a spatial-design problem: equipment and stations were placed without analysing the movement patterns required during peak service. Unlike FM043 (equipment reliability) which targets whether machines work, this FM targets whether their physical arrangement allows efficient human movement between them.

failure_modes.name: Ticket time and service speed failure
failure_modes.description: Average drink preparation time exceeds acceptable thresholds during peak periods, causing downstream queue growth and customer frustration. This is a root cause because ticket-time failure is a workflow-efficiency breakdown driven by the cumulative effect of barista speed, station design, equipment performance, and order complexity. Unlike FM011 (queue management) which observes the customer-facing symptom, this FM targets the behind-the-counter production speed and identifies where seconds are being lost in the drink-build process.

failure_modes.name: Equipment reliability and maintenance failure
failure_modes.description: Key equipment including the espresso machine, grinder, fridge, and dishwasher experiences frequent breakdowns or degraded performance due to absent preventive maintenance. This is a root cause because equipment failure is a maintenance-system gap: machines are run until they break rather than serviced on a schedule. Unlike FM041 (workflow layout) which targets spatial arrangement, this FM targets mechanical reliability: whether equipment is cleaned, serviced, and replaced on a cycle that prevents unexpected downtime during service.

failure_modes.name: Stock replenishment and par level failure
failure_modes.description: Essential supplies run out during service because there is no par-level system, no reorder triggers, and replenishment is reactive rather than planned. This is a root cause because stock-out failure is a demand-forecasting and par-level gap: the business does not calculate how much of each item it needs per trading day and does not trigger orders before running low. Unlike FM057 (inventory management) which targets the counting and tracking system, this FM targets the operational discipline of maintaining service-ready stock levels throughout each day.

failure_modes.name: Shift handover and communication failure
failure_modes.description: No structured handover process exists between shifts: outgoing staff leave without briefing incoming staff on stock levels, issues, customer situations, or outstanding tasks. This is a root cause because handover failure is a communication-process gap: critical operational context is lost at every shift change, forcing the incoming team to discover problems rather than being briefed on them. Unlike FM032 (team meetings) which targets regular communication forums, this FM targets the specific high-risk transition moment between one team and the next.

failure_modes.name: Seating management and turnover failure
failure_modes.description: No strategy exists for managing table turnover during peak periods, meaning seats are occupied by low-spending customers while queuing customers leave. This is a root cause because seating management failure is a capacity-utilisation gap: the cafe treats all seating as self-service without considering dwell-time economics or peak-versus-quiet dynamics. Unlike FM078 (seating layout and comfort) which targets physical furniture arrangement, this FM targets the operational policy for managing how long tables are occupied and by whom during high-demand periods.

failure_modes.name: Delivery integration and workflow failure
failure_modes.description: Third-party delivery orders disrupt in-store workflow: tablets ping during rush, delivery prep conflicts with dine-in orders, and riders create counter congestion. This is a root cause because delivery integration failure is a workflow-design gap: delivery was added as an additional order channel without redesigning the production flow to accommodate it. Unlike FM017 (takeaway customer experience) which targets the quality of the off-premises product, this FM targets the operational disruption that delivery orders cause to the in-store team and dine-in customers.

failure_modes.name: Waste tracking and operational discipline failure
failure_modes.description: Food waste, coffee waste, and packaging waste are not tracked, measured, or analysed, meaning the cafe has no data to drive waste reduction. This is a root cause because waste-tracking failure is a measurement-system gap: without data on what is wasted, when, and why, the business cannot identify patterns or take corrective action. Unlike FM026 (coffee-specific waste) which targets coffee waste streams, this FM covers the broader operational discipline of recording all waste categories and using that data to reduce cost and environmental impact.

failure_modes.name: Coffee roaster dependency and supply risk
failure_modes.description: The cafe depends on a single coffee roaster with no backup arrangement, creating catastrophic supply risk if that roaster experiences disruption. This is a root cause because single-supplier dependency is a procurement-strategy failure: the business has not diversified its most critical input. Unlike FM058 (general supply chain resilience) which targets contingency planning across all suppliers, this FM specifically targets the unique vulnerability of the coffee supply relationship and the disproportionate impact of losing it.

failure_modes.name: Supplier relationship and management failure
failure_modes.description: Supplier relationships are purely transactional: no negotiation on pricing, no communication about upcoming needs, and no partnership that would provide flexibility during disruption. This is a root cause because transactional supplier management is a relationship-investment failure: the cafe treats suppliers as interchangeable vendors rather than strategic partners. Unlike FM049 (roaster dependency) which targets single-source risk, this FM targets the quality and depth of supplier relationships across all categories and whether they provide resilience, flexibility, and value.

failure_modes.name: Milk and dairy supply failure
failure_modes.description: The milk and dairy supply is unreliable: deliveries are late, quality varies, substitutions happen without notice, and alternative milks are frequently unavailable. This is a root cause because dairy supply failure is a supplier-management and forecasting gap specific to the highest-volume daily consumable in most cafes. Unlike FM050 (general supplier relationships) which covers all supplier interactions, this FM targets the specific operational impact of milk problems, which affect every single coffee drink served.

failure_modes.name: Bakery and food supplier quality failure
failure_modes.description: Bakery and food suppliers deliver inconsistent quality: pastries arrive stale, portion sizes vary, or items do not match the agreed specification. This is a root cause because food supplier quality failure is a specification-and-accountability gap: either the expected quality standard was never defined in writing, or there is no process for rejecting substandard deliveries. Unlike FM051 (milk supply reliability) which targets delivery logistics, this FM targets the quality consistency of bought-in food products and whether the cafe actively manages supplier output.

failure_modes.name: Food cost percentage control failure
failure_modes.description: Food cost percentage exceeds sustainable thresholds (typically 35%+ of food revenue) without the business understanding why or taking corrective action. This is a root cause because food cost failure is a margin-management breakdown: either menu items are not individually costed, portions are uncontrolled, waste is untracked, or supplier prices have drifted without renegotiation. Unlike FM054 (coffee cost control) which targets beverage margins, this FM targets the food-specific cost structure and the discipline of knowing what each plate costs to produce.

failure_modes.name: Coffee cost percentage control failure
failure_modes.description: Coffee cost percentage exceeds sustainable thresholds (typically 25%+ of coffee revenue), driven by uncontrolled waste, premium pricing from the roaster, or inefficient dosing. This is a root cause because coffee cost failure is a yield-management breakdown specific to the cafe's core product. Unlike FM053 (food cost) which targets food margins, this FM targets the coffee-specific cost drivers: bean price per kilo, dose per shot, waste per shift, and whether the business negotiates volume pricing with its roaster.

failure_modes.name: Packaging cost and sustainability failure
failure_modes.description: Takeaway packaging costs are not monitored, rising unchecked as cup, lid, bag, and container prices increase without review or supplier comparison. This is a root cause because packaging cost failure is a procurement-oversight gap: packaging is treated as an incidental expense rather than a managed cost line. Unlike FM053/FM054 (food and coffee cost control) which target ingredient margins, this FM targets the non-ingredient consumable costs that accumulate with every takeaway transaction and erode margin invisibly.

failure_modes.name: Ethical sourcing and provenance failure
failure_modes.description: The cafe cannot verify or communicate the ethical sourcing credentials of its coffee, food, or key ingredients to customers who increasingly expect transparency. This is a root cause because ethical sourcing failure is a procurement-values gap: the business either has not investigated its supply chain ethics or has not built the communication framework to share what it knows. Unlike FM050 (supplier relationships) which targets commercial management, this FM targets the provenance and ethical dimension of purchasing decisions and whether they align with stated brand values.

failure_modes.name: Inventory management and stock control failure
failure_modes.description: No inventory tracking system exists: stock counts are infrequent, quantities on hand are unknown, and ordering is based on guesswork rather than data. This is a root cause because inventory management failure is an information-system gap: without accurate stock data, the business cannot control costs, prevent waste, or ensure availability. Unlike FM044 (par-level replenishment) which targets day-to-day stock readiness, this FM targets the underlying counting, recording, and analysis system that should inform all purchasing decisions.

failure_modes.name: Supply chain resilience and contingency failure
failure_modes.description: No contingency plan exists for key supplier failure, delivery disruption, or supply chain shock, leaving the cafe vulnerable to predictable risks. This is a root cause because supply chain resilience failure is a contingency-planning gap: the business has not identified its critical supply dependencies or developed backup arrangements. Unlike FM049 (single-roaster dependency) which targets one specific supplier risk, this FM targets the broader strategic question of whether the business has thought through what happens when any critical supply is disrupted.

failure_modes.name: Revenue per hour and sales productivity failure
failure_modes.description: [SYMPTOM-FM] Revenue per trading hour is declining, but this is a composite outcome driven by upstream failures in pricing (FM061), transaction value (FM060), customer acquisition (FM073), retention (FM015), and product quality (FM023). This FM is retained as a diagnostic aggregator because revenue-per-hour is the single most important financial health metric for a cafe and owners track it instinctively. When this FM fires, the investigation should decompose whether the problem is fewer customers, lower spend per visit, or both, and then trace to the relevant upstream root cause.

failure_modes.name: Average transaction value growth failure
failure_modes.description: Average transaction value has stagnated or declined because the team does not suggest additions, the menu does not facilitate natural upselling, and there is no commercial awareness at the point of sale. This is a root cause because ATV failure is a sales-behaviour and menu-design gap: the opportunity to increase each transaction exists at every interaction but is not being captured. Unlike FM061 (pricing strategy) which targets the price points themselves, this FM targets the transactional behaviour: whether customers are offered a pastry with their coffee, a larger size, or an add-on.

failure_modes.name: Pricing strategy and review failure
failure_modes.description: Menu pricing has not been reviewed or adjusted in over 12 months, meaning margins have eroded as input costs have risen without corresponding price increases. This is a root cause because pricing failure is a commercial-discipline gap: many cafe owners avoid price increases out of fear rather than analysing their margin position and competitor benchmarks. Unlike FM060 (transaction value) which targets upselling behaviour, this FM targets the base price architecture: whether each item is priced to deliver a sustainable margin given current costs.

failure_modes.name: Financial review and reporting failure
failure_modes.description: No regular financial review rhythm exists: the owner does not review weekly sales, monthly P&L, or quarterly trends, making financial decisions on gut feel rather than data. This is a root cause because financial review failure is a management-discipline gap: the numbers exist in the POS and accounting system but nobody looks at them on a structured cadence. Unlike FM063 (cost monitoring) which targets specific cost lines, this FM targets the broader practice of sitting down regularly to understand the financial health of the business.

failure_modes.name: Cost of goods monitoring failure
failure_modes.description: Cost of goods sold is not tracked or benchmarked against industry standards, meaning the business does not know whether its food and coffee margins are healthy. This is a root cause because COGS monitoring failure is a financial-measurement gap: without tracking what percentage of revenue goes to ingredients, the business cannot identify margin erosion until it becomes a cash-flow crisis. Unlike FM062 (financial review) which targets the review cadence, this FM targets the specific metric of input cost as a percentage of revenue and whether it is actively managed.

failure_modes.name: Payroll cost management failure
failure_modes.description: Payroll costs exceed sustainable thresholds (typically 35%+ of revenue) due to overstaffing during quiet periods, inefficient scheduling, or wage structures that do not flex with demand. This is a root cause because payroll failure is a labour-planning gap: staff hours are not aligned to trading patterns, and the cost of labour is not managed as a variable that should track revenue. Unlike FM038 (rota planning) which targets the scheduling process, this FM targets the financial outcome: whether total labour cost is proportionate to what the cafe earns.

failure_modes.name: Cash flow and budgeting failure
failure_modes.description: Cash flow is managed reactively with no forward forecasting, no budget, and no visibility on upcoming commitments, leaving the business vulnerable to predictable shortfalls. This is a root cause because cash-flow failure is a financial-planning gap: the business lives week to week rather than projecting forward. Unlike FM062 (financial review) which targets the backward-looking rhythm of reviewing past performance, this FM targets the forward-looking discipline of knowing what cash is needed when and whether it will be available.

failure_modes.name: Discount and margin erosion failure
failure_modes.description: Habitual or excessive discounting is eroding profit margins: staff give discounts without authorisation, loyalty schemes are poorly designed, or promotional pricing has become permanent. This is a root cause because discount erosion is a pricing-authority and promotional-discipline failure: the business has not defined who can discount, by how much, and under what circumstances. Unlike FM061 (pricing strategy) which targets the base price structure, this FM targets the systematic leakage of margin through uncontrolled or poorly designed discounting.

failure_modes.name: Third-party delivery margin failure
failure_modes.description: Third-party delivery platform commissions (25-35%) are eroding margins on delivery orders, and the business has no strategy to manage, offset, or reduce this cost. This is a root cause because delivery margin failure is a channel-economics gap: the cafe is generating delivery revenue without understanding whether those orders are actually profitable after commission. Unlike FM047 (delivery workflow disruption) which targets operational impact, this FM targets the financial question of whether delivery orders contribute positively to the business or drain margin.

failure_modes.name: Cash handling and reconciliation failure
failure_modes.description: Cash handling procedures are absent or not followed: tills are not reconciled at end of day, cash deposits are irregular, and discrepancies are not investigated. This is a root cause because cash handling failure is a financial-control gap: without reconciliation discipline, theft, error, and loss go undetected. Unlike FM062 (financial review) which targets overall financial visibility, this FM targets the specific daily discipline of counting cash, matching it to POS records, and depositing it on schedule.

failure_modes.name: Online review and reputation failure
failure_modes.description: [SYMPTOM-FM] Online review scores are declining and negative reviews are accumulating, but reputation is a composite outcome of failures in service (FM013), product quality (FM023), environment (FM077), and complaint handling (FM014). This FM is retained as a diagnostic aggregator because online reviews are the most visible public signal of cafe health and directly influence new customer acquisition. When this FM fires, the investigation should read the actual review content to identify which operational failures customers are reporting rather than treating reputation management as a marketing problem.

failure_modes.name: Social media and visual presence failure
failure_modes.description: Social media accounts are inactive, content quality is poor, posting is sporadic, and the cafe's visual presence does not reflect its actual quality or identity. This is a root cause because social media failure is a content-strategy and resource-allocation gap: either nobody is responsible for social media, or they lack the time, skills, or content plan to maintain it. Unlike FM072 (brand identity) which targets the overall brand definition, this FM specifically targets the digital visual channels where potential customers form their first impression.

failure_modes.name: Google Business and local search failure
failure_modes.description: The Google Business Profile is incomplete, contains outdated information, or reviews are not responded to, reducing the cafe's visibility in local search results. This is a root cause because Google Business failure is a local-SEO management gap: the profile is the most important free marketing tool a cafe has, and neglecting it directly reduces footfall from search. Unlike FM070 (social media) which targets content creation, this FM targets the factual accuracy and engagement management of the Google listing that drives map-based discovery.

failure_modes.name: Brand identity and consistency failure
failure_modes.description: The cafe's brand identity is unclear, inconsistent, or not expressed coherently across signage, packaging, social media, interior design, and customer communication. This is a root cause because brand identity failure is a strategic-definition gap: the owner has not articulated what the cafe stands for, who it is for, and how it should feel, or has not translated that definition into consistent visual and verbal expression. Unlike FM070 (social media) which targets one channel, this FM targets the underlying brand architecture that should inform all channels.

failure_modes.name: Customer acquisition strategy failure
failure_modes.description: No defined strategy exists for attracting new customers beyond relying on footfall and word of mouth, leaving growth to chance rather than intention. This is a root cause because acquisition failure is a marketing-strategy gap: the business has not identified who its target customers are, where they can be reached, or what messages would attract them. Unlike FM074 (community marketing) which targets local engagement specifically, this FM targets the broader question of whether the cafe has any deliberate plan to grow its customer base.

failure_modes.name: Community marketing and engagement failure
failure_modes.description: The cafe has no engagement with local community initiatives, events, markets, or neighbourhood partnerships, missing the most cost-effective marketing channel available. This is a root cause because community marketing failure is a local-engagement gap: cafes are inherently neighbourhood businesses, and those that do not participate in their community lose relevance to competitors who do. Unlike FM073 (acquisition strategy) which targets the overall plan, this FM targets the specific tactic of embedding the cafe in its local community through presence, participation, and partnership.

failure_modes.name: Exterior and signage presentation failure
failure_modes.description: The cafe's exterior, signage, and entrance area are uninviting, poorly maintained, or fail to communicate what the cafe offers to people passing by. This is a root cause because exterior presentation failure is a first-impression design gap: potential customers make a split-second decision about whether to enter based on what they see from the street. Unlike FM076 (interior design) which targets the experience after entry, this FM targets the critical moment before entry: whether the exterior attracts, informs, and welcomes.

failure_modes.name: Interior design and atmosphere failure
failure_modes.description: The interior design, decor, and atmosphere feel dated, incoherent, or misaligned with the cafe's brand positioning and target customer expectations. This is a root cause because interior atmosphere failure is a design-investment and curation gap: the physical environment has not been refreshed, or it was never designed with a coherent concept. Unlike FM079 (lighting and sound) which targets specific sensory elements, this FM targets the overall design vision: furniture, colour, materials, artwork, and whether they create a space customers want to spend time in.

failure_modes.name: Cleanliness and hygiene presentation failure
failure_modes.description: Customer-facing areas including the counter, tables, floor, and toilets are inconsistently clean, with visible standards varying between shifts and team members. This is a root cause because hygiene presentation failure is a cleaning-accountability gap: either cleaning tasks are not assigned to specific people at specific times, or there is no quality-check process. Unlike FM005 (cleaning programme) which targets the food-safety cleaning schedule, this FM targets the visible cleanliness that customers notice and that directly affects their willingness to return and their review scores.

failure_modes.name: Seating layout and comfort failure
failure_modes.description: The seating layout is not optimised for the cafe's customer mix: solo workers cannot find appropriate spots, groups are crammed together, and the furniture does not support how customers actually use the space. This is a root cause because seating layout failure is a spatial-planning gap: the furniture was placed without analysing customer-type distribution (solo, pair, group) or usage patterns (laptop work, quick coffee, social meeting). Unlike FM046 (seating turnover management) which targets the operational policy, this FM targets the physical furniture arrangement and whether it matches customer needs.

failure_modes.name: Lighting sound and ambiance failure
failure_modes.description: Lighting is too bright or too dim, background music is absent or inappropriate, temperature is uncomfortable, and the overall sensory environment detracts from the customer experience. This is a root cause because ambiance failure is a sensory-design and curation gap: these elements were either never deliberately designed or have drifted without maintenance. Unlike FM076 (interior design) which targets the visual environment, this FM targets the non-visual sensory elements: sound level, music selection, lighting warmth, and thermal comfort.

failure_modes.name: Storage and back-of-house organisation failure
failure_modes.description: Back-of-house storage areas are chaotic, overfilled, and disorganised, making it difficult to find stock, rotate inventory, or maintain hygiene standards in non-customer areas. This is a root cause because storage organisation failure is a spatial-management and discipline gap: the back of house was either undersized for current needs or is not maintained with the same rigour as front of house. Unlike FM044 (stock replenishment) which targets availability during service, this FM targets the physical organisation of storage that enables or prevents efficient stock management.

failure_modes.name: POS system utilisation failure
failure_modes.description: The POS system's capabilities are underutilised: reporting features are unused, inventory integration is not configured, and the system operates as little more than a cash register. This is a root cause because POS underutilisation is a technology-adoption gap: the hardware and software are in place but the business has not invested time in learning or configuring the features that would provide operational and financial insight. Unlike FM083 (data analytics) which targets reporting and analysis, this FM targets the specific platform already installed and whether its built-in capabilities are being used.

failure_modes.name: Digital ordering and payment failure
failure_modes.description: Digital ordering, mobile payment, QR-code menus, or click-and-collect options are either not offered or poorly implemented despite customer demand for them. This is a root cause because digital ordering failure is a technology-adoption gap: the cafe has not evaluated which digital channels its customers expect or has attempted them without proper implementation. Unlike FM081 (POS utilisation) which targets the existing system's features, this FM targets the customer-facing digital touchpoints that enable ordering and payment beyond the traditional counter interaction.

failure_modes.name: Data reporting and analytics failure
failure_modes.description: No business reporting, performance dashboards, or analytics are in use, meaning the owner makes decisions without data on sales trends, product mix, peak hours, or customer behaviour. This is a root cause because analytics failure is an information-infrastructure gap: the data exists in the POS and other systems but is not extracted, visualised, or reviewed. Unlike FM062 (financial review) which targets the discipline of reviewing numbers, this FM targets the technical capability: whether the business has the tools and configuration to generate useful reports in the first place.

failure_modes.name: Customer data and CRM failure
failure_modes.description: Customer data including email addresses, visit frequency, and preferences is not systematically captured, preventing targeted marketing and relationship building. This is a root cause because CRM failure is a data-collection and customer-relationship gap: the cafe serves hundreds of customers daily but knows almost nothing about them individually. Unlike FM018 (feedback capture) which targets satisfaction data, this FM targets identity and behavioural data: knowing who your customers are, how often they visit, and what they buy, enabling personalised communication and loyalty.

failure_modes.name: IT infrastructure and cybersecurity failure
failure_modes.description: IT infrastructure is unreliable (wifi drops, POS crashes, payment terminals fail) and no cybersecurity measures protect customer data, POS systems, or business records. This is a root cause because IT failure is an infrastructure-investment and maintenance gap: technology was added incrementally without a coherent plan for reliability, security, or backup. Unlike FM081 (POS utilisation) which targets software features, this FM targets the underlying hardware, connectivity, and security that all digital systems depend on.

failure_modes.name: Business planning and strategy failure
failure_modes.description: No written business plan or strategic direction document exists, meaning the cafe operates without defined goals, target market positioning, or a roadmap for the next 12-36 months. This is a root cause because planning failure is a strategic-discipline gap: the owner is consumed by daily operations and has never committed their vision, financial targets, or growth strategy to paper. Unlike FM089 (competitive awareness) which targets market knowledge, this FM targets the internal question of whether the business knows where it is going and how it plans to get there.

failure_modes.name: Expansion readiness and systems failure
failure_modes.description: The business is expanding (new sites, new channels, new offerings) without the operational systems, documented processes, or management capacity to support scale. This is a root cause because expansion-readiness failure is a systems-maturity gap: what works with the owner present at one site breaks when replicated without them. Unlike FM086 (business planning) which targets strategic direction, this FM targets the operational infrastructure question: whether SOPs, training systems, financial controls, and management structures are robust enough to work without the founder in the room.

failure_modes.name: Revenue diversification failure
failure_modes.description: Revenue is concentrated in a single daypart (typically morning coffee) or a single channel, with no diversification into catering, retail, evening trade, or events. This is a root cause because revenue concentration is a commercial-strategy gap: the business has not explored or developed additional income streams that would reduce dependency on peak-morning coffee sales. Unlike FM091 (market innovation) which targets new product and service ideas, this FM targets the financial risk of relying on a narrow revenue base.

failure_modes.name: Competitive awareness and positioning failure
failure_modes.description: The cafe has little awareness of competitor positioning, pricing, menu offerings, or customer experience, operating in a bubble rather than a competitive market. This is a root cause because competitive blindness is a market-intelligence gap: the owner does not systematically visit, monitor, or benchmark against local competitors. Unlike FM086 (business planning) which targets internal strategy, this FM targets external awareness: knowing what alternatives your customers have and how your offer compares.

failure_modes.name: Succession planning failure
failure_modes.description: No succession plan exists for the owner's eventual departure, retirement, sale, or incapacitation, meaning the business has zero value as a going concern without the founder. This is a root cause because succession failure is an ownership-continuity gap: the business is entirely dependent on the current owner and has no framework for transferring knowledge, relationships, or operational control. Unlike FM037 (owner overwork) which targets current delegation, this FM targets the long-term question of whether the business can survive and transfer beyond the founder.

failure_modes.name: Market development and innovation failure
failure_modes.description: No new products, services, experiences, or market approaches are being developed or tested, leaving the cafe vulnerable to more innovative competitors. This is a root cause because innovation failure is a creative-pipeline gap: the business has no process for generating, testing, or launching new ideas. Unlike FM024 (menu stagnation) which targets the food and drink menu specifically, this FM targets broader market innovation: new services like catering or subscriptions, new experiences like events or classes, and new channels like retail or wholesale.

failure_modes.name: Values articulation and alignment failure
failure_modes.description: The cafe's values have not been articulated, documented, or communicated to the team, meaning there is no shared understanding of what the business stands for beyond making and selling coffee. This is a root cause because values failure is a leadership-communication gap: the owner may have strong personal values but has not translated them into a defined, shared framework. Unlike FM093 (standards consistency) which targets behavioural expectations, this FM targets the foundational question of what the cafe believes in and whether anyone besides the owner could articulate it.

failure_modes.name: Standards consistency and culture failure
failure_modes.description: Service standards and behavioural expectations are inconsistent across the team: different people deliver different levels of quality, and there is no shared definition of what 'good' looks like. This is a root cause because standards-culture failure is an alignment and codification gap: expectations exist in the owner's head but are not documented, trained, or reinforced consistently. Unlike FM092 (values articulation) which targets the 'why,' this FM targets the 'how': the specific observable behaviours and quality standards that every team member should demonstrate.

failure_modes.name: Toxic dynamics and conflict resolution failure
failure_modes.description: Interpersonal tension, gossip, cliques, or unresolved conflict is actively degrading the work environment and affecting team performance and customer experience. This is a root cause because toxic dynamics failure is a conflict-management and leadership gap: either the owner is unaware of the dynamics, unwilling to address them, or lacks the skills to resolve them. Unlike FM036 (morale) which captures general disengagement, this FM targets active interpersonal toxicity that poisons the team from within and requires direct intervention.

failure_modes.name: Sustainability commitment and action failure
failure_modes.description: The cafe has no sustainability policy, environmental commitment, or visible action on waste reduction, ethical sourcing, or environmental impact despite growing customer expectations. This is a root cause because sustainability failure is a values-action gap: the business may agree with sustainability principles but has not translated them into concrete practices, targets, or visible commitments. Unlike FM056 (ethical sourcing) which targets procurement provenance, this FM targets the broader organisational commitment to environmental responsibility across operations, packaging, energy, and waste.

failure_modes.name: Community identity and belonging failure
failure_modes.description: The cafe has not established a sense of community identity or belonging, either internally among the team or externally with its neighbourhood and regular customers. This is a root cause because community identity failure is a relationship-building gap: the cafe functions as a transactional space rather than a gathering place with social meaning. Unlike FM074 (community marketing) which targets outreach tactics, this FM targets the deeper question of whether the cafe has become a meaningful part of its community's social fabric or remains anonymous.

failure_modes.name: Operational discipline and daily routine failure
failure_modes.description: [META-FM PENDING DISSOLUTION] This FM currently absorbs 15 inbound signals across operational discipline topics including daily targets, cash reconciliation, supply ordering, prep lists, waste logs, shift changeovers, maintenance logs, temperature monitoring, deep cleaning, banking, and incident reporting. It functions as a super-attractor that should be dissolved into specific root-cause FMs for each discipline area. In its current form, it is too broad to be actionable: telling a cafe owner they have an 'operational discipline failure' provides no diagnostic value. Pending restructuring, treat activation of this FM as a signal to investigate which of its 15 contributing signals is actually firing.

failure_modes.name: Regulatory compliance and risk management failure
failure_modes.description: The cafe is not compliant with one or more regulatory requirements beyond food safety: health and safety risk assessments, fire safety, insurance coverage, data protection, or business continuity planning. This is a root cause because regulatory compliance failure is a governance-awareness gap: the owner either does not know the full scope of legal requirements or has not allocated time to maintain compliance. Unlike FM010 (EHO readiness) which targets food-safety-specific audit compliance, this FM targets the broader regulatory landscape including HSE, fire, insurance, GDPR, and licensing.

failure_modes.name: Health and safety risk assessment failure
failure_modes.description: No documented health and safety risk assessment exists for the premises, meaning workplace hazards have not been formally identified, evaluated, or mitigated. This is a root cause because risk assessment failure is a legal-compliance and safety-management gap: UK law requires a written risk assessment for businesses with five or more employees, and even smaller cafes need one in practice. Unlike FM098 (general regulatory compliance) which covers the full compliance landscape, this FM specifically targets the HSE risk assessment document and whether it reflects current premises conditions.

failure_modes.name: Fire safety and emergency preparedness failure
failure_modes.description: Fire safety measures are inadequate: the fire risk assessment is absent or outdated, extinguishers are not serviced, escape routes are blocked, or emergency procedures are not posted or practised. This is a root cause because fire safety failure is a life-safety compliance gap: unlike most operational failures which cost money, this one can cost lives. Unlike FM099 (health and safety risk assessment) which targets general workplace hazards, this FM specifically targets fire prevention, detection, escape, and the regulatory requirements of the Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order.

failure_modes.name: Licensing and employment law compliance failure
failure_modes.description: The cafe is not compliant with licensing requirements (premises licence, alcohol licence, music licence) or employment law (contracts, minimum wage, working time regulations). This is a root cause because licensing and employment law failure is a legal-knowledge gap: many cafe owners are unaware of the full scope of their obligations, particularly around employment contracts, holiday accrual, and right-to-work checks. Unlike FM098 (general regulatory compliance) which covers broader risk, this FM specifically targets the licensing and employment-law obligations that carry penalties, prosecution risk, or tribunal exposure.

failure_modes.name: Brand consistency across sites failure
failure_modes.description: Brand expression varies significantly between sites in a multi-site operation: different signage, inconsistent service standards, variable product quality, and no unified customer experience. This is a root cause because brand consistency failure is a multi-site governance gap: the brand standards were never codified into a playbook, or the playbook exists but is not enforced through audits and accountability. Unlike FM072 (single-site brand identity) which targets whether a brand is defined, this FM targets whether it is replicated faithfully across multiple locations.

failure_modes.name: SOP compliance variance across sites failure
failure_modes.description: Standard operating procedures are followed differently at different sites, with each location developing its own informal practices that diverge from the intended standard. This is a root cause because SOP variance failure is a multi-site compliance gap: procedures were documented for one site but not actively enforced, audited, or updated as they are deployed across locations. Unlike FM102 (brand consistency) which targets customer-facing brand expression, this FM targets back-of-house operational procedures and whether they are executed uniformly.

failure_modes.name: Cross-site financial performance variance failure
failure_modes.description: Financial performance varies significantly between comparable sites (more than 15% variance in revenue, margin, or cost ratios) without clear strategic explanation. This is a root cause because cross-site financial variance failure is a performance-management gap: the multi-site business does not have the reporting infrastructure to identify, investigate, and resolve site-level performance differences. Unlike FM062 (single-site financial review) which targets one location's financial discipline, this FM targets the comparative analysis and accountability framework across sites.

failure_modes.name: New site opening process failure
failure_modes.description: No documented playbook or checklist exists for opening new sites, meaning each new opening is reinvented from scratch with repeated mistakes and inconsistent outcomes. This is a root cause because new-site opening failure is a knowledge-codification gap: the business has opening experience but has not captured it in a repeatable process. Unlike FM087 (expansion readiness) which targets whether systems are mature enough to scale, this FM targets the specific project-management framework for the new-site opening process itself.

failure_modes.name: Area manager and regional oversight failure
failure_modes.description: No defined area manager or regional oversight role exists, meaning individual sites operate without regular external quality checks, coaching visits, or performance accountability. This is a root cause because oversight failure is an organisational-design gap: the business has grown beyond the owner's ability to personally supervise every site but has not created the management layer to replace that supervision. Unlike FM103 (SOP compliance variance) which targets procedural drift, this FM targets the human role responsible for detecting and correcting that drift.

failure_modes.name: Franchise compliance and audit failure
failure_modes.description: No regular franchise or multi-site compliance audit programme exists, meaning brand, operational, and financial standards are not systematically verified across locations. This is a root cause because audit failure is a quality-assurance gap: without structured, scheduled compliance checks, standards decay gradually and invisibly. Unlike FM106 (area manager oversight) which targets the ongoing supervisory role, this FM targets the formal audit mechanism: scheduled visits, scored assessments, and documented corrective action requirements.

failure_modes.name: Central procurement and cross-site learning failure
failure_modes.description: Central buying power is not leveraged across sites, and there is no mechanism for sharing best practices, innovations, or operational learnings between locations. This is a root cause because central procurement and knowledge-sharing failure is a multi-site synergy gap: the business incurs the complexity costs of multiple sites without capturing the scale benefits. Unlike FM050 (single-site supplier management) which targets individual relationships, this FM targets whether the multi-site structure delivers procurement savings and cross-pollination of operational improvements.

response_patterns.name: Temperature Monitoring & Cold Chain Response
response_patterns.focus: Verify all fridges and cold storage units are logging temperatures daily. Check the last 7 days of records for gaps or out-of-range readings. Any unit running above 5C or freezer above -18C must be investigated immediately. Move high-risk items to compliant units and discard anything that has been stored out of range without documented justification. Confirm the thermometer is calibrated and placed correctly in each unit. Implement a twice-daily temperature monitoring system with designated responsibility for morning and afternoon checks. Install digital probe thermometers in all cold storage units and create a logbook or digital system that records time temperature and initials. Establish a temperature excursion protocol defining what happens when a fridge goes out of range including escalation notification and stock assessment procedures. Build a temperature monitoring culture where daily checks are automatic and never questioned. Display temperature compliance dashboards in the kitchen area. Review temperature trends monthly to identify equipment that may be failing before it breaks down. Your cold chain should be robust enough to survive unannounced EHO inspection at any time.

response_patterns.name: Allergen Management & PPDS Compliance Response
response_patterns.focus: Audit your allergen information immediately. Every menu item must have the 14 declarable allergens documented and available to customers on request. If you sell any pre-packed for direct sale items check Natasha's Law PPDS compliance now — every wrapped sandwich cake or salad must carry a full ingredient list with allergens emphasised. Fix any gaps today. Create an allergen matrix covering every item on your menu including specials and seasonal items. Implement a system for updating allergen information when recipes or suppliers change. Train every team member to handle allergen enquiries confidently and to escalate when uncertain. Develop a customer allergen dialogue script and ensure it is used consistently. Establish allergen management as a non-negotiable part of your food safety culture. Conduct quarterly allergen audits including mystery customer tests. Your allergen processes should be thorough enough that a customer with a severe allergy feels genuinely safe eating with you. Track allergen near-misses and use them to strengthen your system.

response_patterns.name: Hygiene Handwashing & Cleaning Discipline Response
response_patterns.focus: Observe handwashing practice across your team today. Check that soap and paper towels are stocked at every handwash station and that sinks are accessible and not blocked by equipment. Review your cleaning schedule — if there is no written schedule or it is not being signed off confirm who is responsible for what and when. Address any visible hygiene issues in food preparation areas immediately. Develop a comprehensive cleaning schedule covering daily weekly and deep-clean tasks for every area of the cafe. Assign named responsibility for each task and implement a sign-off system. Install handwashing reminder signage and conduct observed handwashing assessments for all food handlers. Review personal hygiene standards including hair coverings aprons and jewellery policy. Build a hygiene culture where cleanliness is a source of team pride not a burden. Integrate pest control monitoring cleaning audits and personal hygiene standards into a single environmental hygiene programme. Conduct quarterly hygiene culture assessments and celebrate compliance. Your cafe should be visibly spotless at any moment of the day.

response_patterns.name: HACCP & Food Safety Management System Response
response_patterns.focus: Check whether you have a documented HACCP-based food safety management system. If you are using Safer Food Better Business or an equivalent it must be current and actively used not filed away. Identify your critical control points — cooking temperatures cooling times hot holding — and confirm they are being monitored and recorded. Any gaps must be addressed this week. Build or update your HACCP system to cover all food processes in your cafe from goods receipt through storage preparation cooking display and service. Document each critical control point with target parameters monitoring frequency corrective actions and responsible person. Train all kitchen and food preparation staff on the HACCP system and their specific responsibilities within it. Your food safety management system should be a living document that evolves with your menu and operations. Conduct internal food safety audits quarterly and use findings to update procedures. Maintain supplier due diligence records and traceability systems. Aim for consistent EHO rating of 5 and use food safety excellence as a customer trust signal.

response_patterns.name: Food Safety Training & EHO Readiness Response
response_patterns.focus: Check every team member's food safety certification status today. All food handlers must hold a minimum Level 2 Food Hygiene certificate. If any certificates have expired or were never obtained arrange training within the next 14 days. Review your most recent EHO inspection report and confirm all recommendations have been actioned. Create a food safety training matrix showing every team member's certification status expiry date and next renewal. Schedule refresher training before certificates expire. Develop an internal food safety induction that covers your specific procedures not just generic training. Conduct a mock EHO inspection quarterly using the official scoring criteria. Embed food safety training into your cafe's identity. Every new starter should complete food safety induction before handling food. Build an internal food safety champion role with responsibility for standards compliance and continuous improvement. Your EHO readiness should be permanent not a last-minute preparation exercise.

response_patterns.name: Queue Management & Service Speed Response
response_patterns.focus: Measure your peak queue times this week. Time how long customers wait from joining the queue to receiving their order during morning rush (7-10am) and lunchtime peak. If any customer waits more than 5 minutes to order or more than 8 minutes for a coffee something is wrong. Identify the bottleneck immediately — is it the till the espresso machine the food prep or the team capacity? Design a peak period workflow that assigns specific roles: dedicated till operator barista food runner and floor support. Create a peak staffing model based on transaction data showing exactly how many team members you need for each hour of the day. Implement a queue management communication strategy where team members acknowledge waiting customers and provide time estimates. Build a service speed culture where the team takes pride in fast smooth service without sacrificing quality. Track average service times daily and display them to the team. Study your workflow during peak and continuously optimise. Your morning rush should feel energised and efficient not chaotic. Customers should never feel ignored even when you are busy.

response_patterns.name: Order Accuracy & Service Quality Response
response_patterns.focus: Track order accuracy for the next five days. Record every wrong drink incorrect food order or missed modification. If errors exceed 5% of orders the problem is systemic not individual. Check whether your ordering process has clear handoff points between taking the order making the drink and presenting it to the customer. Ensure customer names or order numbers are used consistently. Implement a standardised order-taking process with clear call-out protocols between the till and the bar. Create a drink modifier checklist covering milk alternatives syrups temperature preferences and dietary requirements. Develop a quality check step before handoff where the barista confirms the drink matches the order. Train all team members on your greeting standard and customer interaction expectations. Create a service culture where every customer interaction feels personal and attentive. Track order accuracy as a key metric and celebrate perfect days. Develop your team's ability to remember regular customers and their preferences. Your service quality should be a primary reason customers choose your cafe over alternatives.

response_patterns.name: Customer Retention & Loyalty Response
response_patterns.focus: Pull your sales data and identify your return visit patterns. How many unique customers visit weekly? What is your ratio of regulars to new customers? If you cannot answer these questions you have no retention visibility. Start tracking return visits immediately through your POS system loyalty cards or simple observation. Identify your top 20 regulars and confirm your team knows them by name. Design and implement a loyalty programme that rewards return visits — whether digital stamp card or app-based. Create a regular customer recognition system where baristas greet regulars by name and remember their usual order. Implement a lapsed customer re-engagement process for customers who have not visited in 30 days. Track customer lifetime value not just daily transaction counts. Build a community of loyal customers who feel genuine belonging at your cafe. Your regulars should feel like valued members not just repeat transactions. Track retention metrics monthly and use them to shape your customer experience strategy. The strongest independent cafes have return visit rates above 60% — aim to be among them.

response_patterns.name: Complaint Resolution & Service Recovery Response
response_patterns.focus: Review every complaint or negative interaction from the past month. Do you have a record? If complaints are not being documented they are being lost and the problems are repeating. Ensure every team member knows the immediate response protocol: listen acknowledge apologise and resolve on the spot.
